column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=[Easy food.]
#Text=Off the Island of Talim, in the great Lagoon of Bay, my\r
#Text=boatmen bought for a few cuartos several dozens of fish quite twelve\r
#Text=inches long; and those which they couldn't eat were split open, salted,\r
#Text=and dried by a few hours' exposure to the heat of the sun on the roof\r
#Text=of the boat.
#Text=When the fishermen had parted with their contemplated\r
#Text=breakfast, they stooped down and filled their cooking-vessels with\r
#Text=sand-mussels (paludina costata, 2.a G.), first throwing away the\r
#Text=dead ones from the handfuls they picked up from the bottom of the\r
#Text=shallow water.
#Text=[River's importance.]
#Text=Nearly all the dwellings are built by the water's\r
#Text=edge.
#Text=The river is a natural self-maintaining highway, on which loads\r
#Text=can be carried to the foot of the mountains.
#Text=The huts of the people,\r
#Text=built upon piles, are to be seen thickly scattered about its banks,\r
#Text=and particularly about its broad mouths.
#Text=The appropriateness of\r
#Text=their position is evident, for the stream is at once the very\r
#Text=center of activity and the most convenient spot for the pursuit\r
#Text=of their callings.
#Text=At each tide the takes of fish are more or less\r
#Text=plentiful, and at low-water the women and children may be seen picking\r
#Text=up shell-fish with their toes, for practice has enabled them to use\r
#Text=their toes as deftly as their fingers, or gathering in the sand-crabs\r
#Text=and eatable seaweed.
#Text=[Riverside gaiety.]
#Text=The riverside is a pretty sight when men, women,\r
#Text=and children are bathing and frolicking in the shade of the palm-trees;\r
#Text=and others are filling their water-vessels, large bamboos, which they\r
#Text=carry on their shoulders, or jars, which they bear on their heads;\r
#Text=and when the boys are standing upright on the broad backs of the\r
#Text=carabaos and riding triumphantly into the water.
#Text=[Coco-palms.]
#Text=It is here too that the coco-palm most flourishes, a tree\r
#Text=that supplies not only their food and drink, but also every material\r
#Text=necessary for the construction of huts and the manufacture of the\r
#Text=various articles which they use.
#Text=While the greatest care is necessary\r
#Text=to make those growing further inland bear even a little fruit, the\r
#Text=palm-trees close to the shore, even when planted on wretched soil,\r
#Text=grow plentiful crops without the slightest trouble.
#Text=Has a palm-tree\r
#Text=ever been made to blossom in a hothouse?
#Text=Thomson [56] mentions that\r
#Text=coco-trees growing by the sea-side are wont to incline their stems over\r
#Text=the ocean, the waters of which bear their fruit to desert shores and\r
#Text=islands, and render them habitable for mankind.
#Text=Thus the coco-tree\r
#Text=would seem to play an essential part in the ocean vagabondage of\r
#Text=Malaysia and Polynesia.
#Text=[Nipa-palms.]
#Text=Close to the coco-trees grow clumps of the stunted\r
#Text=nipa-palms, which only flourish in brackish waters; [57] their\r
#Text=leaves furnish the best roof-thatching.
#Text=Sugar, brandy, and vinegar\r
#Text=are manufactured from their sap.
#Text=Three hundred and fifty years ago\r
#Text=Pigafetta found these manufactures in full swing, but nowadays\r
#Text=they seem to be limited to the Philippines.
#Text=Besides these, the\r
#Text=pandanus-tree, from the leaves of which the softest mats are woven,\r
#Text=is always found in near proximity to the shore.
#Text=[Fertile fields.]
#Text=Towards the interior the landscape is covered with\r
#Text=rice-fields, which yearly receive a fresh layer of fertile soil,\r
#Text=washed down from the mountains by the river, and spread over their\r
#Text=surface by the overflowing of its waters; and which in consequence\r
#Text=never require any fertilizer.
#Text=[The carabao.]
#Text=The carabao, the favorite\r
#Text=domestic animal of the Malays, and which they keep especially for\r
#Text=agricultural purposes, prefers these regions to all others.
#Text=It loves\r
#Text=to wallow in the mud, and is not fit for work unless permitted to\r
#Text=frequent the water.
#Text=[Bamboo.]
#Text=Bamboos with luxuriant leafy tops grow plentifully by the\r
#Text=huts in the rice-fields which fringe the banks of the river.
#Text=In my\r
#Text=former sketches of travel I have endeavored to describe how much\r
#Text=this gigantic plant contributes to the comfort and convenience of\r
#Text=tropical life.
#Text=Since then I have become acquainted with many curious\r
#Text=purposes to which it is turned, but to describe them here would be\r
#Text=out of place
#Text=.
#Text=[58] I may be allowed, however, to briefly cite a\r
#Text=few examples showing what numerous results are obtained from simple\r
#Text=means.
#Text=Nature has endowed these splendid plants, which perhaps surpass\r
#Text=all others in beauty, with so many useful qualities, and delivered\r
#Text=them into the hands of mankind so ready for immediate use, that a\r
#Text=few sharp cuts suffice to convert them into all kinds of various\r
#Text=utensils.
#Text=[Strength.]
#Text=The bamboo possesses, in proportion to its\r
#Text=lightness, an extraordinary strength; the result of its round shape,\r
#Text=and the regularity of the joints in its stem.
#Text=The parallel position and\r
#Text=toughness of its fibers render it easy to split, and, when split, its\r
#Text=pieces are of extraordinary pliability and elasticity.
#Text=To the gravelly\r
#Text=soil on which it grows it owes its durability, and its firm, even,\r
#Text=and always clean surface, the brilliancy and color of which improve by\r
#Text=use.
#Text=[Convenience.]
#Text=And finally, it is a great thing for a population\r
#Text=with such limited means of conveyance that the bamboo is to be found\r
#Text=in such abundance in all kinds of localities and of all dimensions,\r
#Text=from a few millimeters to ten or fifteen centimeters in diameter,\r
#Text=even sometimes to twice this amount; and that, on account of its\r
#Text=unsurpassed floating power, it is pre-eminently fitted for locomotion\r
#Text=in a country poor in roads but rich in watercourses.
#Text=A blow with a bolo\r
#Text=is generally enough to cut down a strong stem.
#Text=[Usefulness.]
#Text=If the\r
#Text=thin joints are taken away, hollow stems of different thicknesses can\r
#Text=be slid into one another like the parts of a telescope.
#Text=From bamboos\r
#Text=split in half, gutters, troughs, and roofing tiles can be made.
#Text=Split\r
#Text=into several slats, which can be again divided into small strips and\r
#Text=fibers for the manufacture of baskets, ropes, mats, and fine plaiting\r
#Text=work, they can be made into frames and stands.
#Text=Two cuts in the same\r
#Text=place make a round hole through which a stem of corresponding diameter\r
#Text=can be firmly introduced.
#Text=If a similar opening is made in a second\r
#Text=upright, the horizontal stem can be run through both.
#Text=Gates, closing\r
#Text=perpendicularly or horizontally in frames moving without friction on\r
#Text=a perpendicular or horizontal axis, can be made in this way.
#Text=Two deep cuts give an angular shape to the stem; and when its two sides\r
#Text=are wide enough apart to admit of a cross-stem being placed between\r
#Text=them, they can be employed as roof-ridges or for the framework of\r
#Text=tables and chairs; a quantity of flat split pieces of bamboo being\r
#Text=fastened on top of them with chair-cane.
#Text=These split pieces then\r
#Text=form the seats of the chairs and the tops of the tables, instead of\r
#Text=the boards and large bamboo laths used at other times.
#Text=It is equally\r
#Text=easy to make an oblong opening in a large bamboo in which to fit the\r
#Text=laths of a stand.
#Text=A couple of cuts are almost enough to make a fork, a pair of tongs\r
#Text=or a hook.
#Text=If one makes a hole as big as the end of one's finger in a large\r
#Text=bamboo close under a joint, one obtains by fastening a small piece of\r
#Text=cloth to the open end, a syphon or a filter.
#Text=If a piece of bamboo is\r
#Text=split down to the joint in strips, and the strips be bound together\r
#Text=with others horizontally interlaced, it makes a conical basket.
#Text=If\r
#Text=the strips are cut shorter, it makes a peddler's pack basket.
#Text=If\r
#Text=a long handle is added, and it is filled with tar, it can be used\r
#Text=as a signal torch.
#Text=If shallower baskets of the same dimensions,\r
#Text=but with their bottoms cut off or punched out, are placed inside\r
#Text=these conical ones, the two together make capital snare baskets for\r
#Text=crabs and fish.
#Text=If a bamboo stem be cut off just below the joint,\r
#Text=and its lower edge be split up into a cogged rim, it makes, when the\r
#Text=partition of the joint is punched out, an earth-auger, a fountain-pipe,\r
#Text=and many things of the kind.
#Text=[Palm brandy.]
#Text=About nine o'clock, a.m., I rode from Calauan to Pila,\r
#Text=and thence in a northeasterly direction to Santa Cruz, over even,\r
#Text=broad, and well-kept roads, through a palm-grove a mile long and a\r
#Text=mile and a half broad, which extends down to the very edge of the\r
#Text=lagoons.
#Text=The products of these palm trees generally are not used\r
#Text=for the production of oil but for the manufacture of brandy.
#Text=Their\r
#Text=fruit is not allowed to come to maturity; but the buds are slit open,\r
#Text=and the sweet sap is collected  as it drips from them.
#Text=It is then\r
#Text=allowed to ferment, and subjected to distillation. [66] As the sap\r
#Text=is collected twice a day, and as the blossoms, situated at the top\r
#Text=of the tree, are forty or fifty feet above the ground, bamboos are\r
#Text=fastened horizontally, one above the other, from one tree to another,\r
#Text=to facilitate the necessary ascent and descent.
#Text=The sap collector\r
#Text=stands on the lower cross-piece while he holds on to the upper.
#Text=[Profit in manufacture.]
#Text=I afterwards met a Spaniard in Camarines who,\r
#Text=according to his own account, must have made considerable and easy\r
#Text=gains from these contracts.
#Text=He had bought palm-trees at an average\r
#Text=price of five reals apiece (they usually cost more, though they can\r
#Text=be sometimes purchased for two reals).
#Text=Thirty-five palms will furnish\r
#Text=daily at least thirty-six quarts of tuba (sugar-containing sap), from\r
#Text=which, after fermentation and distillation, six quarts of brandy of\r
#Text=the prescribed strength can be manufactured.
#Text=One man is sufficient to\r
#Text=attend to them, and receives for his trouble half the proceeds.
#Text=The\r
#Text=administration pays six cuartos for a quart of brandy.
#Text=My friend the\r
#Text=contractor was in annual receipt, therefore, from every thirty-five\r
#Text=of his trees, of 360 × 1/2 × 5 cuartos = $40.50.
#Text=As the thirty-five\r
#Text=trees only cost him $21.875, his invested capital brought him in\r
#Text=about 200 per cent.
#Text=[Wine and liquor monopoly a failure.]
#Text=The proceeds of this monopoly\r
#Text=(wines and liquors) were rated at $1,622,810 in the colonial budget for\r
#Text=1861; but its collection was so difficult, and so disproportionately\r
#Text=expensive, that it nearly swallowed up the whole profit.
#Text=It caused\r
#Text=espionage, robberies of all sorts, embezzlement, and bribery on a\r
#Text=large scale.
#Text=The retail of the brandy by officials, who are paid by a\r
#Text=percentage on the consumption, did a good deal to injure the popular\r
#Text=respect for the government.
#Text=Moreover, the imposition of this improper\r
#Text=tax on the most important industry of the country not only crippled\r
#Text=the free trade in palms, but also the manufacture of raw sugar;\r
#Text=for the government, to favor their own monopoly, had forbidden the\r
#Text=sugar manufacturers to make rum from their molasses, which became\r
#Text=in consequence so valueless that in Manila they gave it to their\r
#Text=horses.
#Text=The complaints of the manufacturers at last stirred up the\r
#Text=administration to allow the manufacture of rum; but the palm-brandy\r
#Text=monopoly remained intact.
#Text=The Filipinos now drank nothing but rum,\r
#Text=so that at last, in self-defence, the government entirely abandoned\r
#Text=the monopoly (January, 1864).
#Text=Since that, the rum manufacturers pay\r
#Text=taxes according to the amount of their sale, but not upon the amount\r
#Text=of their raw produce.
#Text=In order to cover the deficit occasioned by\r
#Text=the abandonment of the brandy monopoly, the government has made a\r
#Text=small increase in the poll-tax.
#Text=The practice of drinking brandy has\r
#Text=naturally much increased; it is, however, a very old habit. [67] With\r
#Text=this exception, the measure has had the most favorable consequences.
#Text=[Cacao] The cacao-tree comes from Central America.
#Text=It flourishes\r
#Text=there between the 23rd parallel north and the 20th south latitude;\r
#Text=but it is only at its best in the hottest and dampest climates.
#Text=In\r
#Text=temperate climates, where the thermometer marks less than 23° C.,\r
#Text=it produces no fruit.
#Text=[High quality.]
#Text=It was first imported into the Philippines from\r
#Text=Acapulco; either, according to Camarines, by a pilot called Pedro\r
#Text=Brabo de Lagunas, in 1670; or, according to Samar, by some Jesuits,\r
#Text=during Salcedo's government, between 1663 and 1668.
#Text=Since then\r
#Text=it has spread over the greater part of the Island; and, although\r
#Text=it is not cultivated with any excessive care, its fruit is of\r
#Text=excellent quality.
#Text=The cacao of Albay, if its cheapness be taken into\r
#Text=consideration, may be considered at least equal to that of Caracas,\r
#Text=which is so highly-prized in Europe, and which, on account of its\r
#Text=high price, generally is largely mixed with inferior kinds. [74]\r
#Text=The bushes are usually found in small gardens, close to the houses;\r
#Text=but so great is the native laziness that frequently the berries are\r
#Text=allowed to decay, although the local cacao sells for a higher price\r
#Text=than the imported.
#Text=At Cebu and Negros a little more attention is paid\r
#Text=to its cultivation; [Scanty production.] but it does not suffice to\r
#Text=supply the wants of the colony, which imports the deficiency from\r
#Text=Ternate and Mindanao.
#Text=The best cacao of the Philippines is produced in\r
#Text=the small Island of Maripipi, which lies to the north-west of Leyte;\r
#Text=and it is difficult to obtain, the entire crop generally being long\r
#Text=bespoke.
#Text=It costs about one dollar per liter, whereas the Albay cacao\r
#Text=costs from two to two and a half dollars per "ganta" (three liters).
#Text=[Culture.]
#Text=The natives generally cover the kernels, just as they\r
#Text=are beginning to sprout, with a little earth, and, placing them\r
#Text=in a spirally-rolled leaf, hang them up beneath the roof of their\r
#Text=dwellings.
#Text=They grow very rapidly, and, to prevent their being\r
#Text=choked by weeds, are planted out at very short intervals.
#Text=This\r
#Text=method of treatment is probably the reason that the cacao-trees in\r
#Text=the Philippines never attain a greater height than eight or ten feet,\r
#Text=while in their native soil they frequently reach thirty, and sometimes\r
#Text=even forty feet.
#Text=The tree begins to bear fruit in its third or fourth\r
#Text=year, and in its fifth or sixth it reaches maturity, when it usually\r
#Text=yields a "ganta" of cacao, which, as I have mentioned, is worth from\r
#Text=two to two and a half dollars, and always finds a purchaser. [75]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Diseases and pests.]
#Text=The plant is sometimes attacked by a disease,\r
#Text=the origin of which is unknown, when it suffers severely from certain\r
#Text=noxious insects. [77] It is also attacked by rats and other predatory\r
#Text=vermin; the former sometimes falling upon it in such numbers that\r
#Text=they destroy the entire harvest in a single night.
#Text=Travellers in\r
#Text=America say that a well-kept cacao plantation is a very picturesque\r
#Text=sight.
#Text=In the Philippines, however, or at any rate in East Luzon,\r
#Text=the closely-packed, lifeless-looking, moss-covered trees present a\r
#Text=dreary spectacle.
#Text=Their existence is a brief one.
#Text=Their oval leaves,\r
#Text=sometimes nearly a foot long, droop singly from the twigs, and form\r
#Text=no luxuriant masses of foliage.
#Text=Their blossoms are very insignificant;\r
#Text=they are of a reddish-yellow, no larger than the flowers of the lime,\r
#Text=and grow separately on long weedy stalks.
#Text=The fruit ripens in six\r
#Text=months.
#Text=When it is matured, it is of either a red or a yellow tint,\r
#Text=and is somewhat like a very rough gherkin.
#Text=Only two varieties appear to\r
#Text=be cultivated in the Philippines. [78] The pulp of the fruit is white,\r
#Text=tender, and of an agreeable acid taste, and contains from eighteen\r
#Text=to twenty-four kernels, arranged in five rows.
#Text=These kernels are as\r
#Text=large as almonds, and, like them, consist of a couple of husks and a\r
#Text=small core.
#Text=This is the cacao bean; which, roasted and finely ground,\r
#Text=produces cacao, and with the addition of sugar, and generally of\r
#Text=spice, makes chocolate.
#Text=Till the last few years, every household\r
#Text=in the Philippines made its own chocolate, of nothing but cacao\r
#Text=and sugar.
#Text=The natives who eat chocolate often add roasted rice to\r
#Text=it.
#Text=Nowadays there is a manufactory in Manila, which makes chocolate\r
#Text=in the European way.
#Text=The inhabitants of the eastern provinces are\r
#Text=very fond of adding roasted pili nuts to their chocolate. [79]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Chocolate.]
#Text=Europeans first learnt to make a drink from cacao in\r
#Text=Mexico, where the preparation was called chocolatl. [80] Even so far\r
#Text=back as the days of Cortes, who was a tremendous chocolate drinker,\r
#Text=the cacao-tree was extensively cultivated.
#Text=The Aztecs used the beans\r
#Text=as money; and Montezuma used to receive part of his tribute in this\r
#Text=peculiar coin.
#Text=It was only the wealthy among the ancient Mexicans\r
#Text=who ate pure cacao; the poor, on account of the value of the beans\r
#Text=as coins, used to mix maize and mandioca meal with them.
#Text=Even in our\r
#Text=own day the inhabitants of Central America make use of the beans as\r
#Text=small coins, as they have no copper money, nor smaller silver coins\r
#Text=than the half-real.
#Text=Both in Central America and in Orinoco there yet\r
#Text=are many unpenetrated forests which are almost entirely composed of\r
#Text=wild cacao-trees.
#Text=I believe the natives gather some of their fruit,\r
#Text=but it is almost worthless.
#Text=By itself it has much less flavor than the\r
#Text=cultivated kinds.
#Text=Certainly it is not picked and dried at the proper\r
#Text=season, and it gets spoilt in its long transit through the damp woods.
#Text=[An uncertain venture.]
#Text=Since the abolition of slavery, the crops in\r
#Text=America have been diminishing year by year, and until a short time ago,\r
#Text=when the French laid out several large plantations in Central America,\r
#Text=were of but trifling value.
#Text=According to F.
#Text=Engel, a flourishing\r
#Text=cacao plantation required less outlay and trouble, and yields more\r
#Text=profit than any other tropical plant; yet its harvests, which do not\r
#Text=yield anything for the first five or six years, are very uncertain,\r
#Text=owing to the numerous insects which attack the plants.
#Text=In short,\r
#Text=cacao plantations are only suited to large capitalists, or to very\r
#Text=small cultivators who grow the trees in their own gardens.
#Text=Moreover,\r
#Text=as we have said, since the abolition of slavery most of the plantations\r
#Text=have fallen into decay, for the freed slaves are entirely wanting\r
#Text=in industry.
#Text=[Use in Europe.]
#Text=The original chocolate was not generally relished\r
#Text=in Europe.
#Text=When, however, at a later period, it was mixed with sugar,\r
#Text=it met with more approbation.
#Text=The exaggerated praise of its admirers\r
#Text=raised a bitter opposition amongst the opponents of the new drink;\r
#Text=and the priests raised conscientious scruples against the use of so\r
#Text=nourishing an article of food on fast days.
#Text=The quarrel lasted till\r
#Text=the seventeenth century, by which time cacao had become an everyday\r
#Text=necessity in Spain.
#Text=It was first introduced into Spain in 1520; but\r
#Text=chocolate, on account of the monopoly of the Conquistadores, was for a\r
#Text=long time secretly prepared on the other side of the ocean.
#Text=In 1580,\r
#Text=however, it was in common use in Spain, though it was so entirely\r
#Text=unknown in England that, in 1579, an English captain burnt a captured\r
#Text=cargo of it as useless.
#Text=It reached Italy in 1606, and was introduced\r
#Text=into France by Anne of Austria.
#Text=The first chocolate-house in London\r
#Text=was opened in 1657, and in 1700 Germany at last followed suit. [81]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Coffee.]
#Text=The history of coffee in the Philippines is very similar\r
#Text=to that of cacao.
#Text=The plant thrives wonderfully, and its berry has\r
#Text=so strongly marked a flavor that the worst Manila coffee commands as\r
#Text=high a price as the best Java.
#Text=In spite of this, however, the amount\r
#Text=of coffee produced in the Philippines is very insignificant, and,\r
#Text=until lately, scarcely deserved mention.
#Text=According to the report of an\r
#Text=Englishman in 1828, the coffee-plant was almost unknown forty years\r
#Text=before, and was represented only by a few specimens in the Botanical\r
#Text=Gardens at Manila.
#Text=It soon, however, increased and multiplied, thanks\r
#Text=to the moderation of a small predatory animal (paradoxurus musanga),\r
#Text=which only nibbled the ripe fruit, and left the hard kernels (the\r
#Text=coffee beans) untouched, as indigestible.
#Text=The Economical Society\r
#Text=bestirred itself in its turn by offering rewards to encourage the\r
#Text=laying out of large coffee plantations.
#Text=In 1837 it granted to M. de\r
#Text=la Gironnière a premium of $1,000, for exhibiting a coffee plantation\r
#Text=of sixty thousand plants, which were yielding their second harvest;\r
#Text=and four premiums to others in the following year.
#Text=But as soon as\r
#Text=the rewards were obtained the plantations were once more allowed to\r
#Text=fall into neglect.
#Text=From this it is pretty evident that the enterprise,\r
#Text=in the face of the then market prices and the artificially high rates\r
#Text=of freight, did not afford a sufficient profit.
#Text=[Exports.]
#Text=In 1856 the exports of coffee were not more than seven\r
#Text=thousand piculs; in 1865 they had increased to thirty-seven thousand,\r
#Text=five hundred and eighty-eight; and in 1871, to fifty-three thousand,\r
#Text=three hundred and seventy.
#Text=This increase, however, affords no criterion\r
#Text=by which to estimate the increase in the number of plantations,\r
#Text=for these make no returns for the first few years after being laid\r
#Text=out.
#Text=In short, larger exports may be confidently expected.
#Text=But even\r
#Text=greatly increased exports could not be taken as correct measures\r
#Text=of the colony's resources.
#Text=Not till European capital calls large\r
#Text=plantations into existence in the most suitable localities will the\r
#Text=Philippines obtain their proper rank in the coffee-producing districts\r
#Text=of the world.
#Text=[Highest grades.]
#Text=The best coffee comes from the provinces of\r
#Text=Laguna, Batangas and Cavite; the worst from Mindanao.
#Text=The latter,\r
#Text=in consequence of careless treatment, is very impure, and generally\r
#Text=contains a quantity of bad beans.
#Text=The coffee beans of Mindanao are\r
#Text=of a yellowish-white color and flabby; those of Laguna are smaller,\r
#Text=but much firmer in texture.
#Text=[French preference.]
#Text=Manila coffee is very highly esteemed by\r
#Text=connoisseurs, and is very expensive, though it is by no means so\r
#Text=nice looking as that of Ceylon and other more carefully prepared\r
#Text=kinds.
#Text=It is a remarkable fact that in 1865 France, which imported\r
#Text=only $21,000 worth of hemp from the Philippines, imported more than\r
#Text=$200,000 worth of Manila coffee, a third of the entire coffee produce\r
#Text=of the Islands. [82] Manila coffee is not much prized in London,\r
#Text=and does not fetch much more than good Ceylon ($15 per cwt.). [83]\r
#Text=This, however, is no reproach to the coffee, as every one acquainted\r
#Text=with an Englishman's appreciation of coffee will allow.
#Text=[Prices.]
#Text=California, an excellent customer, always ready to give\r
#Text=a fair price for a good article, will in time become one of its\r
#Text=principal consumers. [84] In 1868, coffee in Manila itself cost\r
#Text=an average of $16 per picul. [85] In Java, the authorities pay the\r
#Text=natives, who are compelled to cultivate it, about $3.66 per picul.
#Text=[Philippine exports.]
#Text=Although the amount of coffee exported from the\r
#Text=Philippines is trifling in comparison with the producing powers of the\r
#Text=colony, it compares favorably with the exports from other countries.
#Text=[Javan and Ceylon crops.]
#Text=In my Sketches of Travel, I compared the\r
#Text=decrease of the coffee produced in Java under the forced system of\r
#Text=cultivation with the increase of that voluntarily grown in Ceylon,\r
#Text=and gave the Javanese produce for 1858 as sixty-seven thousand tons,\r
#Text=and the Cingalese as thirty-five thousand tons.
#Text=Since that time the\r
#Text=relative decrease and increase have continued; and in 1866 the Dutch\r
#Text=Indies produced only fifty-six thousand tons, and Ceylon thirty-six\r
#Text=thousand tons. [86]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Amateur scientists.]
#Text=During my enforced stay in Daraga the natives\r
#Text=brought me mussels and snails for sale; and several of them wished\r
#Text=to enter my service, as they felt "a particular vocation for Natural\r
#Text=History."
#Text=At last my kitchen was always full of them.
#Text=They sallied\r
#Text=forth every day to collect insects, and as a rule were not particularly\r
#Text=fortunate in their search; but this was of no consequence; in fact,\r
#Text=it served to give them a fresh appetite for their meals.
#Text=Some of the\r
#Text=neighboring Spaniards paid me almost daily visits; and several of\r
#Text=the native and mestizo dignitaries from a distance were good enough\r
#Text=to call upon me, not so much for the purpose of seeing my humble self\r
#Text=as of inspecting my hat, the fame of which had spread over the whole\r
#Text=province.
#Text=It was constructed in the usual judicious mushroom shape,\r
#Text=covered with nito, [87] and its pinnacle was adorned with a powerful\r
#Text=oil lamp, furnished with a closely fitting lid, like that of a dark\r
#Text=lantern, so that it could be carried in the pocket.
#Text=This last was\r
#Text=particularly useful when riding about on a dark night.
#Text=[Daraga market.]
#Text=Monday and Friday evenings were the Daraga market\r
#Text=nights, and in fine weather always afforded a pretty sight.
#Text=The\r
#Text=women, neatly and cleanly clad, sat in long rows and offered their\r
#Text=provisions for sale by the light of hundreds of torches; and, when\r
#Text=the business was over, the slopes of the mountains were studded all\r
#Text=over with flickering little points of brightness proceeding from the\r
#Text=torches carried by the homeward-bound market women.
#Text=Besides eatables,\r
#Text=many had silks and stuffs woven from the fibers of the pine-apple\r
#Text=and the banana for sale.
#Text=These goods they carried on their heads;\r
#Text=and I noticed that all the younger women were accompanied by their\r
#Text=sweethearts, who relieved them of their burdens.
#Text=[Pineapple fiber preparations.]
#Text=Here I saw them preparing the fiber\r
#Text=of the pine-apple for weaving.
#Text=The fruit of the plants selected\r
#Text=for this purpose is generally removed early; a process which causes\r
#Text=the leaves to increase considerably both in length and in breadth.
#Text=A\r
#Text=woman places a board on the ground, and upon it a pine-apple-leaf with\r
#Text=the hollow side upwards.
#Text=Sitting at one end of the board, she holds\r
#Text=the leaf firmly with her toes, and scrapes its outer surface with a\r
#Text=potsherd; not with the sharp fractured edge but with the blunt side\r
#Text=of the rim; and thus the leaf is reduced to rags.
#Text=In this manner a\r
#Text=stratum of coarse longitudinal fiber is disclosed, and the operator,\r
#Text=placing her thumb-nail beneath it, lifts it up, and draws it away\r
#Text=in a compact strip; after which she scrapes again until a second\r
#Text=fine layer of fiber is laid bare.
#Text=Then, turning the leaf round, she\r
#Text=scrapes its back, which now lies upwards, down to the layer of fiber,\r
#Text=which she seizes with her hand and draws at once, to its full length,\r
#Text=away from the back of the leaf.
#Text=When the fiber has been washed, it is\r
#Text=dried in the sun.
#Text=It is afterwards combed, with a suitable comb, like\r
#Text=women's hair, sorted into four classes, tied together, and treated like\r
#Text=the fiber of the lupi.
#Text=In this crude manner are obtained the threads\r
#Text=for the celebrated web nipis de [Piña.]
#Text=Piña, which is considered by\r
#Text=experts the finest in the world.
#Text=Two shirts of this kind are in the\r
#Text=Berlin Ethnographical Museum (Nos. 291 and 292).
#Text=Better woven samples\r
#Text=are in the Gewerbe Museum of Trade and Commerce.
#Text=In the Philippines,\r
#Text=where the fineness of the work is best understood and appreciated,\r
#Text=richly-embroidered costumes of this description have fetched more\r
#Text=than $1,400 each. [102]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Rice land production.]
#Text=A quiñon of the best rice land is worth from\r
#Text=sixty to one hundred dollars ($5.50 to $9 per acre).
#Text=Rice fields on\r
#Text=rising grounds are dearest, as they are not exposed to devastating\r
#Text=floods as are those in the plain, and may be treated so as to insure\r
#Text=the ripening of the fruit at the time when the highest price is to\r
#Text=be obtained.
#Text=[The harvest.]
#Text=A ganta of rice is sufficient to plant four topones\r
#Text=(1 topon = 1 loan); from which 100 manojos (bundles) are gathered,\r
#Text=each of which yields half a ganta of rice.
#Text=The old ganta of Naga,\r
#Text=however, being equal to a modern ganta and a half, the produce\r
#Text=may be calculated at 75 cavanes per quiñon, about 9 3/4 bushels per\r
#Text=acre. [109] In books 250 cavanes are usually stated to be the average\r
#Text=produce of a quiñon; but that is an exaggeration.
#Text=The fertility of\r
#Text=the fields certainly varies very much; but, when it is considered\r
#Text=that the land in the Philippines is never fertilized, but depends,\r
#Text=for the maintenance of its vitality, exclusively upon the overflowing\r
#Text=of the mud which is washed down from the mountains, it may be believed\r
#Text=that the first numbers better express the true average.
#Text=In Java the\r
#Text=harvest, in many provinces, amounts to only 50 cavanes per quiñon;\r
#Text=in some, indeed, to three times this amount; and in China, with the\r
#Text=most careful culture and abundant manure, to 180 cabanes
#Text=.
#Text=[Sweet potatoes.]
#Text=Besides rice, they cultivate the camote (sweet\r
#Text=potato, Convolvulus batatas).
#Text=This flourishes like a weed; indeed,\r
#Text=it is sometimes planted for the purpose of eradicating the weeds from\r
#Text=soil intended for coffee or cacao.
#Text=It spreads out into a thick carpet,\r
#Text=and is an inexhaustible storehouse to its owner, who, the whole year\r
#Text=through, can supply his wants from his field.
#Text=Gabi (Caladium), Ubi\r
#Text=(Dioscorea), maize, and other kinds of grain, are likewise cultivated.
#Text=[Cattle and horses.]
#Text=After the rice harvest the carabaos, horses, and\r
#Text=bullocks, are allowed to graze in the fields.
#Text=During the rice culture\r
#Text=they remain in the gogonales, cane-fields which arise in places once\r
#Text=cultivated for mountain-rice and afterwards abandoned.
#Text=(Gogo is the\r
#Text=name of a cane 7 to 8 feet high, Saccharum sp.).
#Text=Transport then is\r
#Text=almost impossible, because during the rainy season the roads are\r
#Text=impassable, and the cattle find nothing to eat.
#Text=The native does\r
#Text=not feed his beast, but allows it to die when it cannot support\r
#Text=itself.
#Text=In the wet season of the year it frequently happens that a\r
#Text=carabao falls down from starvation whilst drawing a cart.
#Text=A carabao\r
#Text=costs from $7 to $10; a horse $10 to $20; and a cow $6 to $8.
#Text=Very fine\r
#Text=horses are valued at from $30 to $50, and occasionally as much as $80;\r
#Text=but the native horses are not esteemed in Manila, because they have no\r
#Text=stamina.
#Text=The bad water, the bad hay, and the great heat of the place at\r
#Text=once point out the reason; otherwise it would be profitable to export\r
#Text=horses in favorable seasons to Manila, where they would fetch twice\r
#Text=their value.
#Text=According to Morga, there were neither horses nor asses\r
#Text=on the Island until the Spaniards imported them from China and New\r
#Text=Spain. [111] They were at first small and vicious.
#Text=Horses were imported\r
#Text=also from Japan, "not swift but powerful, with large heads and thick\r
#Text=manes, looking like Friesland horses;" [112] and the breed improved\r
#Text=rapidly.
#Text=Those born in the country, mostly cross-breeds, drive well.
#Text=[Black cattle.]
#Text=Black cattle are generally in the hands of a few\r
#Text=individuals; some of whom in Camarines possess from 1000 to 3000 head;\r
#Text=but they are hardly saleable in the province, although they have been\r
#Text=exported profitably for some years past to Manila.
#Text=The black cattle\r
#Text=of the province are small but make good beef.
#Text=They are never employed\r
#Text=for labor, and the cows are not milked.
#Text=The Filipinos, who generally\r
#Text=feed on fish, crabs, mussels, and wild herbs together with rice,\r
#Text=prefer the flesh of the carabao to that of the ox; but they eat it\r
#Text=only on feastdays.
#Text=[Sheep.]
#Text=The old race of sheep, imported by the Spaniards previous\r
#Text=to this century, still flourishes and is easily propagated.
#Text=Those\r
#Text=occasionally brought from Shanghai and Australia are considered to be\r
#Text=deficient in endurance, unfruitful, and generally short-lived.
#Text=Mutton\r
#Text=is procurable every day in Manila; in the interior, however, at\r
#Text=least in the eastern provinces, very rarely; although the rearing\r
#Text=of sheep might there be carried on without difficulty, and in many\r
#Text=places most profitably; the people being too idle to take care of the\r
#Text=young lambs, which they complain are torn to pieces by the dogs when\r
#Text=they wander about free.
#Text=The sheep appear to have been acclimatized\r
#Text=with difficulty.
#Text=Morga says that they were brought several times\r
#Text=from New Spain, but did not multiply; so that in his time this kind\r
#Text=of domestic animal did not exist.
#Text=[Swine.]
#Text=Pork is eaten by wealthy\r
#Text=Europeans only when the hog has been brought up from the litter at\r
#Text=home.
#Text=In order to prevent its wandering away, it is usually enclosed\r
#Text=in a wide meshed cylindrical hamper of bamboo, upon filling which\r
#Text=it is slaughtered.
#Text=The native hogs are too nauseous for food, the\r
#Text=animals maintaining themselves almost entirely on ordure.
#Text=[Meals.]
#Text=The Filipino eats three times a day--about 7 a.m., 12, and at\r
#Text=7 or 8 in the evening.
#Text=Those engaged in severe labor consume at each\r
#Text=meal a chupa of rice; the common people, half a chupa at breakfast, one\r
#Text=at mid-day, and half again in the evening, altogether two chupas.
#Text=Each\r
#Text=family reaps its own supply of rice, and preserves it in barns, or\r
#Text=buys it winnowed at the market; in the latter case purchasing only\r
#Text=the quantity for one day or for the individual meals.
#Text=The average\r
#Text=retail price is 3 cuartos for 2 chupas (14 chupas for 1 real).
#Text=To\r
#Text=free it from the husk, the quantity for each single meal is rubbed in\r
#Text=a mortar by the women.
#Text=This is in accordance with an ancient custom;\r
#Text=but it is also due to the fear lest, otherwise, the store should be\r
#Text=too quickly consumed.
#Text=The rice, however, is but half cooked; and\r
#Text=it would seem that this occurs in all places where it constitutes\r
#Text=an essential part of the sustenance of the people, as may be seen,\r
#Text=indeed, in Spain and Italy.
#Text=Salt and much Spanish pepper (capsicum)\r
#Text=are eaten as condiments; the latter, originally imported from America,\r
#Text=growing all round the houses.
#Text=To the common cooking-salt the natives\r
#Text=prefer a so-called rock-salt, which they obtain by evaporation from\r
#Text=sea-water previously filtered through ashes; and of which one chinanta\r
#Text=(12 lbs.
#Text=German) costs from one and one-half to two reals.
#Text=The\r
#Text=consumption of salt is extremely small.
#Text=[Household affairs.]
#Text=The details of domestic economy may be summarized\r
#Text=as follows:\r
#Text=\r
#Text=For cooking purposes an earthen pot is used, costing between 3 and 10\r
#Text=cuartos; which, in cooking rice, is closed firmly with a banana-leaf,\r
#Text=so that the steam of a very small quantity of water is sufficient.
#Text=No\r
#Text=other cooking utensils are used by the poorer classes; but those better\r
#Text=off have a few cast-iron pans and dishes.
#Text=In the smaller houses, the\r
#Text=hearth consists of a portable earthen pan or a flat chest, frequently\r
#Text=of an old cigar-* chest full of sand, with three stones which serve\r
#Text=as a tripod.
#Text=In the larger houses it is in the form of a bedstead,\r
#Text=filled with sand or ashes, instead of a mattress.
#Text=The water in small\r
#Text=households is carried and preserved in thick bamboos.
#Text=In his bolo\r
#Text=(forest-knife), moreover, every one has an universal instrument,\r
#Text=which he carries in a wooden sheath made by himself, suspended by a\r
#Text=cord of loosely-twisted bast fibers tied round his body.
#Text=This, and\r
#Text=the rice-mortar (a block of wood with a suitable cavity), together\r
#Text=with pestles and a few baskets, constitute the whole of the household\r
#Text=[Furniture.] furniture of a poor family; sometimes a large snail,\r
#Text=with a rush wick, is also to be found as a lamp.
#Text=They sleep on a\r
#Text=mat of pandanus (fan-palm, Corypha), when they possess one; if not,\r
#Text=on the splittings of bamboo, with which the house is floored.
#Text=By the\r
#Text=poor oil for lighting is rarely used; but torches of resin, which\r
#Text=last a couple of days, are bought in the market for half a cuarto.
#Text=[Clothing.]
#Text=Their clothing requirements I ascertained to be these:\r
#Text=A woman wears a camisa de guinára (a short shift of abacá fiber),\r
#Text=a patadíon (a gown reaching from the hip to the ancles), a cloth,\r
#Text=and a comb.
#Text=A piece of guinára, costing 1 real, gives two shifts;\r
#Text=the coarsest patadíon costs 3 reals; a cloth, at the highest, 1 real;\r
#Text=and a comb, 2 cuartos; making altogether 4 reals, 12 cuartos.
#Text=Women of\r
#Text=the better class wear a camisa, costing between 1 and 2 r., a patadíon\r
#Text=6 r., cloth between 2 and 3 r., and a comb 2 cu.
#Text=The men wear a shirt,\r
#Text=1 r., hose, 3 r., hat (tararura) of Spanish cane, 10 cu., or a salacot\r
#Text=(a large rain-hat, frequently decorated), at least 2 r.
#Text=--often,\r
#Text=when ornamented with silver, as much as $50.
#Text=At least three, but more\r
#Text=commonly four, suits are worn out yearly; the women, however, taking\r
#Text=care to weave almost the whole quantity for the family themselves.
#Text=[Wages.]
#Text=The daily wages of the common laborer are 1 real, without\r
#Text=food; and his hours of work are from 6 to 12, and from 2 to 6\r
#Text=o'clock.
#Text=The women, as a rule, perform no field labor, but plant out\r
#Text=the rice and assist in the reaping; their wages on both occasions\r
#Text=being equal to those of the men.
#Text=Wood and stone-cutters receive 1.5\r
#Text=r. per day, and calkers 1.75 r.
#Text=[Land leases.]
#Text=The Tercio is a pretty general contract in the\r
#Text=cultivation of the land.
#Text=The owner simply lets arable land for the\r
#Text=third part of the crop.
#Text=Some mestizos possess several pieces of ground;\r
#Text=but they are seldom connected together, as they generally acquire\r
#Text=them as mortgages for sums bearing but a small proportion to their\r
#Text=real value.
#Text=[Family income.]
#Text=Under the head of earnings I give the income of a\r
#Text=small family.
#Text=The man earns daily one real, and the woman, if she\r
#Text=weaves coarse stuff, one-fourth real, and her food (thus a piece\r
#Text=of guinára, occupying the labor of two days, costs half a real in\r
#Text=weavers' wages).
#Text=The most skilful female weaver of the finer stuffs\r
#Text=obtains twelve reals per piece; but it takes a month to weave; and\r
#Text=the month, on account of the numerous holy-days, must be calculated\r
#Text=at the most as equal to twenty-four working days; she consequently\r
#Text=earns one-fourth real per day and her food.
#Text=For the knitting of the\r
#Text=fibers of the ananas for the piña web (called sugot) she gets only\r
#Text=an eighth of a real and her food.
#Text=[Schools.]
#Text=In all the pueblos there are schools.
#Text=The schoolmaster\r
#Text=is paid by the Government, and generally obtains two dollars per\r
#Text=month, without board or lodging.
#Text=In large pueblos the salary amounts\r
#Text=to three dollars and a half; out of which an assistant must be\r
#Text=paid.
#Text=The schools are under the supervision of the ecclesiastics\r
#Text=of the place.
#Text=Reading and writing are taught, the writing copies\r
#Text=being Spanish.
#Text=The teacher, who has to teach his scholars Spanish\r
#Text=exactly, does not understand it himself, while the Spanish officers,\r
#Text=on the other hand, do not understand the language of the country;\r
#Text=and the priests have no inclination to alter this state of things,\r
#Text=which is very useful to them as a means of influence.
#Text=Almost the only\r
#Text=Filipinos who speak Spanish are those who have been in the service\r
#Text=of Europeans.
#Text=A kind of religious horn-book is the first that is\r
#Text=read in the language of the country (Bicol); and after that comes the\r
#Text=Christian Doctrine, the reading-book called Casayayan.
#Text=On an average,\r
#Text=half of all the children go to school, generally from the seventh\r
#Text=to the tenth year.
#Text=They learn to read a little; a few even write a\r
#Text=little: but they soon forget it again.
#Text=Only those who are afterwards\r
#Text=employed as clerks write fluently; and of these most write well.
#Text=Some priests do not permit boys and girls to attend the same school;\r
#Text=and in this case they pay a second teacher, a female, a dollar a\r
#Text=month.
#Text=The Filipinos learn arithmetic very quickly, generally aiding\r
#Text=themselves by the use of mussels or stones, which they pile in little\r
#Text=heaps before them and then count through.
#Text=[Marriage age.]
#Text=The women seldom marry before the fourteenth year,\r
#Text=twelve years being the legal limit.
#Text=In the church-register of Polángui\r
#Text=I found a marriage recorded (January, 1837) between a Filipino and a\r
#Text=Filipina having the ominous name of Hilaria Concepción, who at the\r
#Text=time of the performance of the marriage ceremony was, according to\r
#Text=a note in the margin, only nine years and ten months old.
#Text=Frequently\r
#Text=people live together unmarried, because they cannot pay the expenses\r
#Text=of the ceremony. [115]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Woman's work.]
#Text=European females, and even mestizas, never seek\r
#Text=husbands amongst the natives.
#Text=The women generally are well treated,\r
#Text=doing only light work, such as sewing, weaving, embroidery, and\r
#Text=managing the household; while all the heavy labor, with the exception\r
#Text=of the beating of the rice, falls to the men. [116]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[A patriarch.]
#Text=Instances of longevity are frequent amongst the\r
#Text=Filipinos, particularly in Camarines.
#Text=The Diario de Manila, of\r
#Text=March 13th, 1866, mentions an old man in Darága (Albay) whom I knew\r
#Text=well--Juan Jacob, born in 1744, married in 1764, and a widower\r
#Text=in 1845.
#Text=He held many public posts up to 1840, and had thirteen\r
#Text=children, of whom five are living.
#Text=He has one hundred and seventy\r
#Text=direct descendants, and now, at one hundred and twenty-two years of\r
#Text=age, is still vigorous, with good eyes and teeth.
#Text=Extreme unction\r
#Text=was administered to him seven times!
#Text=[Snake bite and rabies remedy.]
#Text=The first excretion of a new-born\r
#Text=child is carefully preserved, and under the name of triaca (theriacum)\r
#Text=is held to be a highly efficacious and universal remedy for the bites\r
#Text=of snakes and mad dogs.
#Text=It is applied to the wound externally, and\r
#Text=at the same time is taken internally.
#Text=[Infant mortality.]
#Text=A large number of children die in the first two\r
#Text=weeks after birth.
#Text=Statistical data are wanting; but, according to the\r
#Text=opinion of one of the first physicians in Manila, at least one-fourth\r
#Text=die.
#Text=This mortality must arise from great uncleanliness and impure air;\r
#Text=since in the chambers of the sick, and of women lying-in, the doors\r
#Text=and windows are so closely shut that the healthy become sick from\r
#Text=the stench and heat, and the sick recover with difficulty.
#Text=Every\r
#Text=aperture of the house is closed up by the husband early during\r
#Text=travail, in order that Patianac may not break in--an evil spirit\r
#Text=who brings mischief to lying-in women, and endeavors to hinder the\r
#Text=birth.
#Text=The custom has been further maintained even amongst many\r
#Text=who attach no belief to the superstition, but who, from fear of a\r
#Text=draught of air through a hole, have discovered a new explanation for\r
#Text=an old custom--namely, that instances of such practices occur amongst\r
#Text=all people.
#Text=[The itch.]
#Text=One very widely-spread malady is the itch,\r
#Text=although, according to the assurance of the physician above referred\r
#Text=to, it may be easily subdued; and, according to the judgment of those\r
#Text=who are not physicians and who employ that term for any eruptions\r
#Text=of the skin, the natives generally live on much too low a diet; the\r
#Text=Bicols even more than the Tagalogs. [117] Under certain conditions,\r
#Text=which the physicians, on being questioned, could not define more\r
#Text=precisely, the natives can support neither hunger nor thirst; of which\r
#Text=fact I have on many occasions been a witness.
#Text=It is reported of them,\r
#Text=when forced into such a situation as to suffer from unappeased wants,\r
#Text=that they become critically ill; and thus they often die.
#Text=[Imitation mania.]
#Text=Hence arises the morbid mania for imitation,\r
#Text=which is called in Java Sakit-latar, and here Mali-mali.
#Text=In Java many\r
#Text=believe that the sickness is only assumed, because those who pretend\r
#Text=to be afflicted with it find it to their advantage to be seen by newly\r
#Text=arrived Europeans.
#Text=Here, however, I saw one instance where indeed no\r
#Text=simulation could be suspected.
#Text=My companions availed themselves of\r
#Text=the diseased condition of a poor old woman who met us in the highway,\r
#Text=to practice some rough jokes upon her.
#Text=The old woman imitated every\r
#Text=motion as if impelled by an irresistible impulse, and expressed at\r
#Text=the same time the most extreme indignation against those who abused\r
#Text=her infirmity.
#Text=[The sickness in Siberia.]
#Text=In R.
#Text=Maak's "Journey to the Amour," it is\r
#Text=recorded:--"It is not unusual for the Maniagri to suffer also from a\r
#Text=nervous malady of the most peculiar kind, with which we had already\r
#Text=been made acquainted by the descriptions of several travellers. [118]\r
#Text=This malady is met with, for the most part, amongst the wild people\r
#Text=of Siberia, as well as amongst the Russians settled there.
#Text=p. 39); and lovers at parting exchange pieces of the linen they may\r
#Text=be wearing, and during their separation inhale the odor of the beloved\r
#Text=being, besides smothering the relics with kisses. [121]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Indang.]
#Text=I returned down the stream in a boat to Indang, a\r
#Text=comparatively flourishing place, of smaller population but more\r
#Text=considerable trade than Daet; the export consisting principally of\r
#Text=abacá, and the import of rice.
#Text=[Storms.]
#Text=An old mariner, who had navigated this coast for many\r
#Text=years, informed me that the same winds prevail from Daet as far as\r
#Text=Cape Engaño, the north-east point of Luzon.
#Text=From October to March\r
#Text=the north-east wind prevails, the monsoon here beginning with north\r
#Text=winds, which are of short duration and soon pass into the north-east;\r
#Text=and in January and February the east winds begin and terminate\r
#Text=the monsoon.
#Text=The heaviest rains fall from October to January, and\r
#Text=in October typhoons sometimes occur.
#Text=Beginning from the north or\r
#Text=north-east, they pass to the north-west, where they are most violent;\r
#Text=and then to the north and east, sometimes as far as to the south-east,\r
#Text=and even to the south.
#Text=In March and April, and sometimes in the\r
#Text=beginning of May, shifting winds blow, which bring in the south-west\r
#Text=monsoon; but the dry season, of which April and May are the driest\r
#Text=months, is uninterrupted by rain.
#Text=Thunder storms occur from June to\r
#Text=November; most frequently in August.
#Text=During the south-west monsoon\r
#Text=the sea is very calm; but in the middle of the north-east monsoon all\r
#Text=navigation ceases on the east coast.
#Text=In the outskirts of Baler rice\r
#Text=is sown in October, and reaped in March and April.
#Text=Mountain rice is\r
#Text=not cultivated.
#Text=[A petition for liquors.]
#Text=As the Igorots were not permitted to\r
#Text=have cocoa-palms for the preparation of wine, vinegar and brandy,\r
#Text=so that they might not infringe the monopoly of the government,\r
#Text=they presented me with a petition entreating me to obtain this\r
#Text=favor for them.
#Text=The document was put together by a Filipino writer\r
#Text=in so ludicrously confused a manner that I give it as a specimen of\r
#Text=Philippine clerkship. [152] At all events, it had the best of results,\r
#Text=for the petitioners were accorded twice as much as they had prayed for.
#Text=[Winds and planting season.]
#Text=The south-west monsoon lasts in this\r
#Text=region (district of Goa) from April to October.
#Text=April is very calm\r
#Text=(navegación de señoras).
#Text=From June to August the south-west winds\r
#Text=blow steadily; March, April, and May are the driest months; there are\r
#Text=shifting winds in March and the beginning of April; while from October\r
#Text=to December is the time of storms; "S.
#Text=Diaphor." p. 363) says:--"In India it has been employed as a remedy\r
#Text=against cholera under the name of Papecta."
#Text=Papecta is probably a\r
#Text=clerical error.
#Text=In K.
#Text=Lall Dey's "Indigenous Drugs of India," it is\r
#Text=called Papeeta, which is pronounced Pepita in English; and Pepita is\r
#Text=the Spanish word for the kernel of a fruit.
#Text=It is also held in high\r
#Text=estimation as an antidote for the bite of serpents.
#Text=Father Blanco\r
#Text=("Flora of the Philippines," 61), states that he has more than once\r
#Text=proved its efficacy in this respect in his own person; but he cautions\r
#Text=against its employment internally, as it had been fatal in very many\r
#Text=cases.
#Text=It should not be taken into the mouth, for should the spittle\r
#Text=be swallowed, and vomiting not ensue, death would be inevitable.
#Text=The\r
#Text=parish priest of Tabaco, however, almost always carried a pepita in\r
#Text=his mouth.
#Text=From 1842 he began occasionally to take an Ignatius bean\r
#Text=into his mouth as a protection against cholera, and so gradually\r
#Text=accustomed himself to it.
#Text=When I met him in 1860 he was quite well,\r
#Text=and ascribed his health and vigor expressly to that habit.
#Text=According to\r
#Text=his communication, in cases of cholera the decoction was successfully\r
#Text=administered in small doses introduced into tea; but it was most\r
#Text=efficacious when, mixed with brandy, it was applied as a liniment.
#Text=[Superstitions regarding the "Bisayan" bean.]
#Text=Huc also ("Thibet,"\r
#Text=I. 252) commends the expressed juice of the kouo-kouo (Faba\r
#Text=Ign. amar.) both for internal and external use, and remarks that it\r
#Text=plays a great part in Chinese medicine, no apothecary's shop being\r
#Text=without it.
#Text=Formerly the poisonous drug was considered a charm, as\r
#Text=it is still by many.
#Text=Father Camel [185] states that the Catbalogan\r
#Text=or Bisayan-bean, which the Indians call Igasur or Mananaog (the\r
#Text=victorious), was generally worn as an amulet round the neck, being\r
#Text=a preservative against poison, contagion, magic, and philtres, so\r
#Text=potent, indeed, that the Devil in propia persona could not harm the\r
#Text=wearer.
#Text=Especially efficacious is it against a poison communicated by\r
#Text=breathing upon one, for not only does it protect the wearer, but it\r
#Text=kills the individual who wishes to poison him.
#Text=Camel further mentions\r
#Text=a series of miracles which superstition ascribed to the Ignatius bean.
#Text=[Coconuts.]
#Text=On the southern half of the eastern border, on the shore\r
#Text=from Borongan by Lauang as far as Guiuan, there are considerable\r
#Text=plantations of cocos, which are most imperfectly applied to the\r
#Text=production of oil.
#Text=From Borongan and its visitas twelve thousand\r
#Text=pitchers of coconut oil are yearly exported to Manila, and the nuts\r
#Text=consumed by men and pigs would suffice for at least eight thousand\r
#Text=pitchers.
#Text=As a thousand nuts yield eight pitchers and a half, the\r
#Text=vicinity of Borongan alone yields annually six million nuts; for\r
#Text=which, assuming the average produce at fifty nuts, one hundred-twenty\r
#Text=thousand fullbearing palms are required.
#Text=The statement that their\r
#Text=number in the above-mentioned district amounts to several millions\r
#Text=must be an exaggeration.
#Text=[Getting coco oil.]
#Text=The oil is obtained in a very rude manner.
#Text=The\r
#Text=kernel is rasped out of the woody shell of the nut on rough boards,\r
#Text=and left to rot; and a few boats in a state of decay, elevated on posts\r
#Text=in the open air, serve as reservoirs, the oil dropping through their\r
#Text=crevices into pitchers placed underneath; and finally the boards are\r
#Text=subjected to pressure.
#Text=This operation, which requires several months\r
#Text=for its completion, yields such a bad, dark-brown, and viscid product\r
#Text=that the pitcher fetches only two dollars and a quarter in Manila,\r
#Text=while a superior oil costs six dollars. [186]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Oil factory.]
#Text=Recently a young Spaniard has erected a factory\r
#Text=in Borongan for the better preparation of oil.
#Text=A winch, turned by\r
#Text=two carabaos, sets a number of rasps in motion by means of toothed\r
#Text=wheels and leather straps.
#Text=They are somewhat like a gimlet in form,\r
#Text=and consist of five iron plates, with dentated edges, which are\r
#Text=placed radiating on the end of an iron rod, and close together,\r
#Text=forming a blunt point towards the front.
#Text=The other end of the rod\r
#Text=passes through the center of a disk, which communicates the rotary\r
#Text=motion to it, and projects beyond it.
#Text=The workman, taking a divided\r
#Text=coconut in his two hands, holds its interior arch, which contains the\r
#Text=oil-bearing nut, with a firm pressure against the revolving rasp, at\r
#Text=the same time urging with his breast, which is protected by a padded\r
#Text=board, against the projecting end of the rod.
#Text=The fine shreds of the\r
#Text=nut remain for twelve hours in flat pans, in order that they may be\r
#Text=partially decomposed.
#Text=They are then lightly pressed in hand-presses;\r
#Text=and the liquor, which consists of one-third oil and two-thirds water,\r
#Text=is caught in tubs, from which, at the end of six hours, the oil,\r
#Text=floating on the surface, is skimmed off.
#Text=It is then heated in iron\r
#Text=pans, containing 100 liters, until the whole of the water in it has\r
#Text=evaporated, which takes from two to three hours.
#Text=In order that the\r
#Text=oil may cool rapidly, and not become dark in color, two pailfuls of\r
#Text=cold oil, freed from water, are poured into it, and the fire quickly\r
#Text=removed to a distance.
#Text=The compressed shreds are once more exposed\r
#Text=to the atmosphere, and then subjected to a powerful pressure.
#Text=After\r
#Text=these two operations have been twice repeated, the rasped substance\r
#Text=is suspended in sacks between two strong vertical boards and crushed\r
#Text=to the utmost by means of clamp screws, and repeatedly shaken up.
#Text=The\r
#Text=refuse serves as food for pigs.
#Text=The oil which runs from the sacks is\r
#Text=free from water, and is consequently very clear, and is employed in\r
#Text=the cooling of that which is obtained in the first instance. [187]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Limited output.]
#Text=The factory produces fifteen hundred tinajas of\r
#Text=oil.
#Text=It is in operation only nine months in the year; from December to\r
#Text=February the transport of nuts being prevented by the tempestuous seas,\r
#Text=there being no land communication.
#Text=The manufacturer was not successful\r
#Text=in procuring nuts from the immediate vicinity in sufficient quantity\r
#Text=to enable him to carry on his operations without interruption, nor,\r
#Text=during the favorable season of the year, could he lay up a store for\r
#Text=the winter months, although he paid the comparatively high price of\r
#Text=three dollars per thousand.
#Text=[Illogical business.]
#Text=While the natives manufactured oil in the manner\r
#Text=just described, they obtained from a thousand nuts three and a half\r
#Text=pots, which, at six reals each, fetched twenty-one reals; that is three\r
#Text=reals less than was offered them for the raw nuts.
#Text=These data, which\r
#Text=are obtained from the manufacturers, are probably exaggerated, but\r
#Text=they are in the main well founded; and the traveller in the Philippines\r
#Text=often has the opportunity of observing similar anomalies.
#Text=For example,\r
#Text=in Daet, North Camarines, I bought six coconuts for one cuarto, at\r
#Text=the rate of nine hundred and sixty for one dollar, the common price\r
#Text=there.
#Text=On my asking why no oil-factory had been erected, I received\r
#Text=for answer that the nuts were cheaper singly than in quantities.
#Text=In\r
#Text=the first place, the native sells only when he wants money; but he\r
#Text=knows that the manufacturer cannot well afford to have his business\r
#Text=suspended; so, careless of the result, he makes a temporary profit,\r
#Text=and never thinks of ensuring for himself a permanent source of income.
#Text=[Sugar venders.]
#Text=In the province of Laguna, where the natives prepare\r
#Text=coarse brown sugar from sugar-cane, the women carry it for leagues to\r
#Text=the market, or expose it for sale on the country roads, in small loaves\r
#Text=(panoche), generally along with buyo.
#Text=Every passenger chats with the\r
#Text=seller, weighs the loaf in the hand, eats a bit, and probably passes\r
#Text=on without buying any.
#Text=In the evening the woman returns to her home\r
#Text=with her wares, and the next day repeats the same process.
#Text=[Rice-farming.]
#Text=The culture of rice follows the seasons of the year.
#Text=In\r
#Text=some places where there are large fields the plough (arado) and the\r
#Text=sod-sod (here called surod) are employed; but, almost universally, the\r
#Text=rice-field is only trodden over by carabaos in the rainy season.
#Text=Sowing\r
#Text=is done on the west coast in May and June, planting in July and August,\r
#Text=and reaping from November to January.
#Text=One ganta of seed-corn gives two,\r
#Text=sometimes from three to four, cabanes (i.e., fifty, seventy-five,\r
#Text=and a hundred fold).
#Text=In the chief town, Catbalogan, there are but\r
#Text=very few irrigated fields (tubigan, from tubig, water), the produce\r
#Text=of which does not suffice for the requirements, and the deficiency\r
#Text=is made up from other places on the coasts of the Island.
#Text=On the\r
#Text=other hand, Catbalogan produces abaca, coconut oil, wax, balate\r
#Text=(edible holothuria, sea cucumber), dried fish, and woven stuffs.
#Text=On\r
#Text=the north and east coasts sowing takes place from November to January,\r
#Text=and reaping six months later.
#Text=During the remaining six months the field\r
#Text=serves as pasture for the cattle; but in many places rice culture goes\r
#Text=on even during these months, but on other fields.
#Text=A large portion of\r
#Text=this rice is frequently lost on account of the bad weather.
#Text=[Mountain rice.]
#Text=Mountain-rice culture, which in Catbalogan is almost\r
#Text=the only cultivation, requires no other implement of agriculture\r
#Text=than the bolo to loosen the soil somewhat, and a sharp stick for\r
#Text=making holes at distances of six inches for the reception of five or\r
#Text=six grains of rice.
#Text=Sowing is done from May to June, weeding twice,\r
#Text=and five months later it is cut stalk by stalk; the reaper receiving\r
#Text=half a real daily wages and food.
#Text=The produce is between two and three\r
#Text=cabanes per ganta, or fifty to seventy fold.
#Text=The land costs nothing,\r
#Text=and wages amount to nearly five reals per ganta of seed-corn.
#Text=After a\r
#Text=good harvest the caban fetches four reales; but just before the harvest\r
#Text=the price rises to one dollar, and often much higher.
#Text=The ground is\r
#Text=used only once for dry rice; camote (batata), abacá, and caladium being\r
#Text=planted on it after the harvest.
#Text=Mountain rice is more remunerative\r
#Text=than watered rice about in the proportion of nine to eight.
#Text=[Other products.]
#Text=Next to rice the principal articles of sustenance\r
#Text=are camote (convolvulus batatas), ubi (dioscorea), gabi (caladium),\r
#Text=palauan (a large arum, with taper leaves and spotted stalk).
#Text=Camote\r
#Text=can be planted all the year around, and ripens in four months; but\r
#Text=it takes place generally when the rice culture is over, when little\r
#Text=labor is available.
#Text=When the cultivation of camote is retained,\r
#Text=the old plants are allowed to multiply their runners, and only the\r
#Text=tubers are taken out of the ground.
#Text=But larger produce is obtained by\r
#Text=cleaning out the ground and planting anew.
#Text=From eighteen to fifteen\r
#Text=gantas may be had for half a real.
#Text=[Abacá.]
#Text=Although there are large plantations of abacá, during my\r
#Text=visit it was but little cultivated, the price not being sufficiently\r
#Text=remunerative.
#Text=[Tobacco.]
#Text=Tobacco also is cultivated.
#Text=Formerly it might be sold in\r
#Text=the country, but now it has to be delivered to the government.
#Text=[Balao oil.]
#Text=A resinous oil (balao or malapajo) is found in Samar\r
#Text=and Albay, probably also in other provinces.
#Text=It is obtained from a\r
#Text=dipterocarpus (apiton), one of the loftiest trees of the forest, by\r
#Text=cutting in the trunk a wide hole, half a foot deep, hollowed out into\r
#Text=the form of a basin, and from time to time lighting a fire in it, so as\r
#Text=to free the channels, through which it flows, of obstructions.
#Text=The oil\r
#Text=thus is collected daily and comes into commerce without any further\r
#Text=preparation.
#Text=Its chief application is in the preservation of iron\r
#Text=in shipbuilding.
#Text=Nails dipped in the oil of the balao, before being\r
#Text=driven in, will, as I have been assured by credible individuals,\r
#Text=defy the action of rust for ten years; but it is principally used as a\r
#Text=varnish for ships, which are painted with it both within and without,\r
#Text=and it also protects wood against termites and other insects.
#Text=The\r
#Text=balao is sold in Albay at four reals for the tinaja of ten gantas (the\r
#Text=liter at eight pence).
#Text=A cement formed by the mixture of burnt lime,\r
#Text=gum elemi, and coconut oil, in such proportions as to form a thick\r
#Text=paste before application, is used for the protection of the bottoms\r
#Text=of ships; and the coating is said to last a year. [199] [Wax.]
#Text=Wax\r
#Text=is bartered by the Cimarronese.
#Text=The whole of Samar annually yields\r
#Text=from two hundred to three hundred piculs, whose value ranges between\r
#Text=twenty-five and fifty dollars per picul, while in Manila the price\r
#Text=is generally five to ten dollars higher; but it fluctuates very much,\r
#Text=as the same product is brought from many other localities and at very\r
#Text=irregular intervals of time.
#Text=[Scarcity of stock.]
#Text=There is hardly any breeding of cattle,\r
#Text=notwithstanding the luxuriant growth of grasses and the absence of\r
#Text=destructive animals.
#Text=Horses and carabao are very rare, and are said\r
#Text=to have been introduced late, not before the present century.
#Text=As in\r
#Text=Samar there are hardly any other country roads than the seashore and\r
#Text=the shallow beds of rivers (it is better in the north of Leyte),\r
#Text=the carabao is used only once every year in treading over the\r
#Text=earth of the rice-field.
#Text=During the year he roams at large on the\r
#Text=pastures, in the forest, or on a small island, where such exists,\r
#Text=in the neighborhood.
#Text=Some times in the year one may see several\r
#Text=carabaos, attached to the large trunk of a tree, dragging it to the\r
#Text=village.
#Text=Their number, consequently, is extremely small.
#Text=Carabaos\r
#Text=which tread the rice land well are worth as much as ten dollars.
#Text=The\r
#Text=mean price is three dollars for a carabao, and five to six dollars\r
#Text=for a caraballa.
#Text=Horned cattle are only occasionally used as victims\r
#Text=at festivals.
#Text=The property of several owners, they are very limited in\r
#Text=number, and live half-wild in the mountains.
#Text=There is hardly any trade\r
#Text=in them, but the average price is three dollars for a heifer, and five\r
#Text=or six dollars for a cow.
#Text=[Swine.]
#Text=Almost every family possesses a pig;\r
#Text=some, three or four of them.
#Text=A fat pig costs six or seven dollars,\r
#Text=even more than a cow.
#Text=Many Filipino tribes abstain strictly from beef;\r
#Text=but pork is essential to their feasts.
#Text=Grease, too, is so dear that\r
#Text=from three to four dollars would, under favorable circumstances,\r
#Text=be got on that account for a fat animal.
#Text=[Sheep and goats.]
#Text=Sheep\r
#Text=and goats thrive well, and propagate easily, but also exist only in\r
#Text=small numbers, and are hardly utilized either for their wool or their\r
#Text=flesh.
#Text=Creoles and mestizos are for the most part too idle even to\r
#Text=keep sheep, preferring daily to eat chicken.
#Text=The sheep of Shanghai,\r
#Text=imported by the governor of Tacloban, also thrive and propagate\r
#Text=famously.
#Text=[Poultry.]
#Text=A laying hen costs half a real, a rooster the\r
#Text=same, and a game cock as much as three dollars, often considerably\r
#Text=more.
#Text=Six or eight hens, or thirty eggs, may be bought for one real.
#Text=[Cost of food.]
#Text=A family consisting of father, mother, and five\r
#Text=children requires daily nearly twenty-four chupas of palay (rice in\r
#Text=the husk), which, after winnowing, comes to about twelve chupas.
#Text=This\r
#Text=at the average price of four reals per cavan costs about half a\r
#Text=real.
#Text=The price, however, varies.
#Text=Sometimes, after the harvest, it\r
#Text=is three reals per cavan; before it, ten; and in Albay, even about\r
#Text=thirty reals.
#Text=Then about three cuartos are wanted for extras (as fish,\r
#Text=crabs, vegetables, etc.), which, however, are generally collected\r
#Text=by the children; and, lastly, for oil two cuartos, buyo one cuarto,\r
#Text=tobacco three cuartos (three leaves for one cuarto), the latter being\r
#Text=smoked, not chewed.
#Text=A woman consumes half as much buyo and tobacco\r
#Text=as a man.
#Text=Buyo and tobacco are less used in Leyte than in Samar.
#Text=[Sugar.]
#Text=The higher prices which can be obtained for directly\r
#Text=exported sugar, combined with the facility and security of the\r
#Text=trade as contrasted with the late monopoly enjoyed by Manila, have\r
#Text=occasioned a great extension of the cultivation of that article.
#Text=Not\r
#Text=only in Iloilo, but also in Antique and Negros, many new plantations\r
#Text=have arisen, and the old ones have been enlarged as much as possible;\r
#Text=and not less important has been the progress in the manufacture.
#Text=In\r
#Text=1857 there was not one iron mill to be found on the island; so that,\r
#Text=in working with the wooden mill, about thirty per cent. of the sap\r
#Text=remained in the cane, even after it had thrice passed through.
#Text=The\r
#Text=old wooden presses, which were worked by steam or carabaos, have now\r
#Text=been supplanted by new ones; and these the native planters have no\r
#Text=difficulty in obtaining, as they can get them on credit from the\r
#Text=warehouses of the English importers.
#Text=Instead of the old Chinese\r
#Text=cast-iron pans which were in use, far superior articles have been\r
#Text=imported from Europe; and many large factories worked by steam-power\r
#Text=and with all modern improvements have been established.
#Text=In agriculture,\r
#Text=likewise, creditable progress is noticeable.
#Text=Improved ploughs, carts,\r
#Text=and farming implements generally, are to be had in plenty.
#Text=These\r
#Text=changes naturally show how important it was to establish at different\r
#Text=points, extending over two hundred miles of the Archipelago, commercial\r
#Text=centers, where it was desirable that foreigners should settle.
#Text=Without\r
#Text=these latter, and the facilities afforded to credit which thereby\r
#Text=ensued, the sudden rise and prosperity of Iloilo would not have been\r
#Text=possible, inasmuch as the mercantile houses in that capital would have\r
#Text=been debarred from trading with unknown planters in distant provinces,\r
#Text=otherwise than for ready money.
#Text=A large number of half-castes, too,\r
#Text=who before traded in manufactured goods purchased in Manila, were\r
#Text=enabled after this to send their goods direct to the provinces, to the\r
#Text=foreign firms settled there; and as, ultimately, neither these latter\r
#Text=nor the Chinese retail dealers could successfully compete with them,\r
#Text=the result has been that, as much to their own profit as to that of the\r
#Text=country, they have betaken themselves to the cultivation of sugar.
#Text=In\r
#Text=this manner important plantations have been established in Negros,\r
#Text=which are managed by natives of Iloilo: but there is a scarcity of\r
#Text=laborers on the island.
#Text=[Banana varieties.]
#Text=In Albay there are about twelve varieties\r
#Text=of the best banana cultivated, which are particularly favored by\r
#Text=the qualities of the soil.
#Text=The cultivation is extremely simple,\r
#Text=and entirely independent of the seasons.
#Text=The plants thrive best on\r
#Text=the slopes of the volcanic mountains (in which Albay and Camarines\r
#Text=abound), in open spaces of the woods protected by the trees, which\r
#Text=cast their shadows to an extent of about sixty feet.
#Text=In exposed level\r
#Text=ground they do not thrive so well, and in marshy land not at all.
#Text=[Cultivation.]
#Text=In the laying out of a new plantation the young shoots\r
#Text=are generally made use of, which sprout so abundantly from the roots\r
#Text=that each individual one soon becomes a perfect plant.
#Text=In favorable\r
#Text=ground the custom is to allow a distance of about ten feet between\r
#Text=each plant; in poor ground six feet.
#Text=The only care necessary is\r
#Text=the extermination of the weeds, and clearing away the undergrowth\r
#Text=during the first season; later on, the plants grow so luxuriantly\r
#Text=and strongly that they entirely prevent the growth of anything\r
#Text=else in their vicinity.
#Text=The protection afforded by the shade of the\r
#Text=trees at this period is no longer required, the young buds finding\r
#Text=sufficient protection against the sun's rays under cover of the\r
#Text=fan-like leaves.
#Text=Only in exceptional cases, contrary to the usual\r
#Text=practice, are the plants raised from seed.
#Text=The fruit, when ready,\r
#Text=is cut off and dried, though care must be taken that it is not over\r
#Text=ripe; otherwise the kernels will not germinate.
#Text=These latter are about\r
#Text=the size of peppercorns; and the extraction of them in the edible\r
#Text=species almost always brings about decay.
#Text=Two days before sowing,\r
#Text=the kernels are taken out of the fruit, and steeped overnight in\r
#Text=water; on the following day they are dried in a shady place; and on\r
#Text=the third day they are sown in holes an inch deep in fresh, unbroken,\r
#Text=and well-shaded forest ground, allowing six inches distance between\r
#Text=each plant and row.
#Text=After a year the seedlings, which are then about\r
#Text=two feet high, are planted out, and tended in the same way as the\r
#Text=suckers.
#Text=[Differences with abacá.]
#Text=While many of the edible bananas\r
#Text=bear fruit after one year, and a few varieties even after six months,\r
#Text=the abacá plant requires on an average three years to produce its\r
#Text=fiber in a proper condition; when raised from suckers four years;\r
#Text=and raised from year-old seedlings, even under the most favorable\r
#Text=conditions, two years.
#Text=[Cutting.]
#Text=On the first crop, only one stalk is cut from each bush;\r
#Text=but later on the new branches grow so quickly that they can be cut\r
#Text=every two months. [221] After a few years the plants become so strong\r
#Text=and dense that it is scarcely possible to push through them.
#Text=Bast is\r
#Text=in its best condition at the time of blossoming; but, when the price\r
#Text=of the fiber happens to stand high in the market, this particular\r
#Text=time is not always waited for.
#Text=[Prejudice against cutting after blossoming.]
#Text=Plants which have\r
#Text=blossomed cease to be profitable in any way, by reason of the fiber\r
#Text=becoming too weak--a matter of too great nicety for the unpractical\r
#Text=consumers on the other side of the Atlantic to decide upon, and one\r
#Text=in which, despite inquiries and careful inspections, they might be\r
#Text=deceived.
#Text=There really is no perceptible reason why the fiber should\r
#Text=become weaker through fructification, which simply consists in the fact\r
#Text=of the contents of the vascular cells changing into soluble matter,\r
#Text=and gradually oozing away, the consequence of which is that the cells\r
#Text=of the fiber are not replenished.
#Text=These, on the contrary, acquire\r
#Text=additional strength with the age of the plant, because the emptied\r
#Text=cells cling so firmly together, by means of a certain resinous deposit,\r
#Text=that it is impossible to obtain them unbroken without a great deal of\r
#Text=trouble.
#Text=The idea may have erroneously arisen from the circumstance\r
#Text=that, previously to drying, as with hemp, the old plants were picked\r
#Text=out, and allowed to be thrown away, though not without considerably\r
#Text=increasing the rate of pay, which already consumed the greater part\r
#Text=of the general expenses. [222]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Extracting the fiber.]
#Text=In order to obtain the bast, the stalk above\r
#Text=ground is closely pruned and freed from leaves and other encumbrances;\r
#Text=each leaf is then singly divided into strips--a cross incision being\r
#Text=made through the membrane on the inner or concave side, and connected\r
#Text=by means of the pulpy parts (the parenchym) clinging together.
#Text=In\r
#Text=this manner as much as possible of the clear outer skin only remains\r
#Text=behind.
#Text=Another method is to strip the bast from the undivided stem.
#Text=To\r
#Text=effect this the operator makes an oblique incision in the skin of\r
#Text=the under part of the stalk, drawing the knife gradually to the tip,\r
#Text=and stripping off the whole length as broad a piece as possible; and\r
#Text=the operation is repeated as many times as practicable.
#Text=This method\r
#Text=of handling is more productive than the one previously described;\r
#Text=but, on the other hand, it takes considerably more time, and for\r
#Text=that reason is not often practised.
#Text=The strips of bast are then drawn\r
#Text=under a knife, the blade of which is three inches broad by six long,\r
#Text=fastened at one end to the extremity of a flexible stick so that it\r
#Text=is suspended perpendicularly over a well-smoothed block, and at the\r
#Text=other end to a handle connected by means of a cord to a treadle, which\r
#Text=can be pressed firmly down, as occasion requires.
#Text=The workman draws\r
#Text=the bast, without any regard to quality, between the knife and block,\r
#Text=commencing in the middle, and then from side to side.
#Text=The knife must\r
#Text=be free from notches, or all indentations, according to the direction\r
#Text=of Father Blanco. [223]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Increasing culture.]
#Text=Whatever may have been the causes of this evident\r
#Text=backwardness, from the period of the establishment of the Philippine\r
#Text=Company in these Islands, and in consequence of the exertions of some\r
#Text=of the directors to promote the cultivation of indigo, at that time\r
