#Text=very little known, the natives have slowly, though gradually, been\r
#Text=reconciled to it; and discovering it to be one of the most advantageous\r
#Text=branches of industry, although accompanied with some labor and exposed\r
#Text=to the influence of droughts and excessive heats, as well as to the\r
#Text=risks attendant on the extraordinary anticipation of the rainy seasons,\r
#Text=have of late years paid more attention to it.
#Text=The quintal of indigo\r
#Text=of the first class costs the planter from $35 to $40 at most; and in\r
#Text=the market of Manila it has been sold from $60 to $130, according to\r
#Text=the quality and the greater or lesser demand for the article at the\r
#Text=season.
#Text=As, however, everything in this colony moves within a small\r
#Text=circle, it is not possible to obtain large quantities for exportation;\r
#Text=not only because of the risk in advancing the Indian sums of money\r
#Text=on account of his crop, but also owing to the annual surplus seldom\r
#Text=exceeding from two to two thousand five hundred distributed in many\r
#Text=hands, and collected by numerous agents, equally interested in making\r
#Text=up their return-cargoes.
#Text=[Sugar.]
#Text=The cultivation of the sugar-cane is more or less extended\r
#Text=to all the provinces of these Islands, owing to its consumption among\r
#Text=the natives being both great and general; but those of La Pampanga and\r
#Text=Pangasinan are more particularly devoted to it.
#Text=These two provinces\r
#Text=alone annually produce about 550,000 arrobas (13,750,000 lbs.) of\r
#Text=which one-third is usually exported in Chinese and other foreign\r
#Text=vessels.
#Text=In extraordinary seasons, the amount exported greatly\r
#Text=exceeds the quantity above stated, as, for example, happened in the\r
#Text=monsoon of 1796, when the planters came down to the port of Manila,\r
#Text=and by contract exported upwards of nine millions weight, of the first\r
#Text=and second qualities.
#Text=The price of this article has experienced many\r
#Text=variations of late years; but the medium may be estimated at $6 for one\r
#Text=hundred twenty-five pounds of the first quality, and $5 for the second.
#Text=[Black pepper.]
#Text=The production is cultivated in the Provinces of\r
#Text=Tayabas, Batangas, and La Laguna, but in such small quantities, that,\r
#Text=notwithstanding the powerful allurements of all kinds constantly held\r
#Text=out by the Royal Company during the long period of twenty years,\r
#Text=their agents have never been able to collect in more than about\r
#Text=64,000 lbs. annually.
#Text=After every encouragement, the most that\r
#Text=has been attained with the natives, is confined to their planting\r
#Text=in some districts fifty to one hundred pepper-vines round their\r
#Text=huts, which they cultivate in the same way as they would plots of\r
#Text=flowers, but without any other labor than supporting the plant with\r
#Text=a proportioned stake, clearing the ground from weeds, and attending\r
#Text=to daily irrigation.
#Text=[A possibility.]
#Text=This article therefore scarcely deserves a place\r
#Text=amongst the flourishing branches of agriculture, at least till it has\r
#Text=been raised from its present depressed state, and the grounds laid\r
#Text=out in regular and productive pepper-groves.
#Text=Till this is done, to\r
#Text=a corresponding extent, it must also be excluded from the number of\r
#Text=productions furnished by these Islands to commerce and exportation;\r
#Text=more particularly if we consider that, notwithstanding the great\r
#Text=fragrance of the grain, as well as its general superiority over the\r
#Text=rest of Asia, so great a difference exists in the actual price, that\r
#Text=this can never be compensated by its greater request in the markets of\r
#Text=Europe, and much less enable it to compete with that of the British and\r
#Text=Dutch, till its abundance has considerably lowered its primitive value.
#Text=[Not popular.]
#Text=Finally, although an infinity of grounds are to be found\r
#Text=adapted to the rapid propagation of pepper-vines, as may easily be\r
#Text=inferred from the analogy and proximity of the Philippine Islands to\r
#Text=the others of this same archipelago, so well known for their growth\r
#Text=of spices, it must be confessed that it is a species of culture by\r
#Text=no means popular among the Philippine natives, and it would be almost\r
#Text=requiring too much from their inconstancy of character, to wish them\r
#Text=to dedicate their lands and time to the raising of a production which,\r
#Text=besides demanding considerable care, is greatly exposed to injury,\r
#Text=and even liable to be destroyed by the severity of the storms, which\r
#Text=frequently mark the seasons.
#Text=With difficulty would they be induced to\r
#Text=wait five years before they were able to gather the uncertain fruits\r
#Text=of their labor and patience.
#Text=If, therefore, it should ever be deemed\r
#Text=a measure of policy to encourage the growth of black pepper, it will\r
#Text=be necessary for the government to order the commons belonging to each\r
#Text=town, and adapted to this species of plantation, to be appropriated to\r
#Text=this use, by imposing on the inhabitants the obligation of taking care\r
#Text=of them, and drawing from the respective coffers of each community the\r
#Text=necessary funds for the payment of the laborers, and the other expenses\r
#Text=of cultivation.
#Text=If this cannot be done, it will be necessary to wait\r
#Text=till the general condition of the country is improved, when through\r
#Text=the spirit of emulation, and the enterprises of the planters being duly\r
#Text=patronized and supported, present difficulties may be overcome, and the\r
#Text=progressive results of future attempts will be then found to combine\r
#Text=the interests of individuals with the general welfare of the colony.
#Text=[Coffee.]
#Text=So choice is the quality of the coffee produced in the\r
#Text=Island of Luzon, especially in the districts of Indang and Silang,\r
#Text=in the province of Cavite, that if it is not equal to that of Mocha,\r
#Text=I at least consider it on parallel with the coffee of Bourbon; but,\r
#Text=as the consumption and cultivation are extremely limited, it cannot\r
#Text=with any propriety be yet numbered among the articles contributing\r
#Text=to the export-trade.
#Text=[Cocoa.]
#Text=Cocoa is something more attended to, in consequence of the\r
#Text=use of chocolate being greatly extended among the natives of easy\r
#Text=circumstances.
#Text=That of the Island of Cebu, is esteemed superior to\r
#Text=the cocoa of Guayaquil, and possibly it is not excelled by that of\r
#Text=Soconusco.
#Text=As, however, the quantity raised does not suffice for\r
#Text=the local consumption, Guayaquil cocoa meets a ready sale, and is\r
#Text=generally brought in return-cargo by the ships coming from Acapulco,\r
#Text=and those belonging to the Philippine company dispatched from Callao,\r
#Text=the shipping port of Lima.
#Text=The cultivation of these two articles in the Philippines is on the\r
#Text=same footing as that of pepper, which, as above stated, is rather an\r
#Text=object of luxury and recreation than one of speculation among the\r
#Text=Filipinos.
#Text=The observations and rules pointed out in the preceding\r
#Text=article, are, in a general sense, applicable to both these branches\r
#Text=of industry.
#Text=[Cinnamon.]
#Text=Cinnamon groves, or trees of wild cinnamon, are to be\r
#Text=found in every province.
#Text=In Mindanao, a Dutchman, some years ago,\r
#Text=was employed by orders of the government, in examining the forests\r
#Text=and making experiments, with a view to discover the same tree of\r
#Text=this species that has given so much renown to Ceylon; but, whether\r
#Text=it was owing to a failure in the discovery, or, when the plant was\r
#Text=found, as at the time was said to be the case, the same results were\r
#Text=not produced, from the want of skill in preparing, or stripping off\r
#Text=the bark; certain it is, that the laudable attempt totally failed,\r
#Text=or rather the only advantage gained, has been the extracting from\r
#Text=the bark and more tender parts of the branches of the tree, an oil\r
#Text=or essence of cinnamon, vigorous and aromatic in the extreme.
#Text=[Experiment in Laguna.]
#Text=About the same time, a land-owner of the name\r
#Text=Salgado, undertook to form an extensive plantation of the same species\r
#Text=in the province of La Laguna, and succeeded in seeing upwards of a\r
#Text=million cinnamon trees thrive and grow to a considerable size; but\r
#Text=at last, he was reluctantly compelled to desist from his enterprise,\r
#Text=by the same reasons which led to the failure of Mindanao.
#Text=[Need of experienced cultivators.]
#Text=These facts are of sufficient\r
#Text=authority for our placing the cinnamon tree among the indigenous\r
#Text=productions of the Philippine Islands and considering their general\r
#Text=excellence above those of the same nature in the rest of Asia, it may\r
#Text=reasonably be concluded that, without the tree being identically the\r
#Text=same, the cinnamon with which it is clothed will be found finer than\r
#Text=that yielded by the native plant of the Island of Ceylon, and this\r
#Text=circumstance, consequently, holds out a hope that, in the course of\r
#Text=time, it may become an article of traffic, as estimable as it would be\r
#Text=new.
#Text=In order, however, that this flattering prospect may be realized,\r
#Text=it will be requisite for the government to procure some families,\r
#Text=or persons from the above island, acquainted with the process of\r
#Text=stripping off the bark and preparing the cinnamon, by dexterously\r
#Text=offering allurements, corresponding to the importance of the service,\r
#Text=which, although in itself it may probably be an extremely simple\r
#Text=operation, as long as it is unknown, will be an insuperable obstacle\r
#Text=to the propagation of so important an agricultural pursuit.
#Text=[Nutmeg.]
#Text=Two species of nutmeg are known here, the one in shape\r
#Text=resembling a pigeon's egg, and the other of a perfectly spherical\r
#Text=form; but both are wild and little aromatic, and consequently held\r
#Text=in no great esteem.
#Text=[Rice.]
#Text=Rice is the bread and principal aliment of these natives, for\r
#Text=which reason, although its cultivation is among the most disagreeable\r
#Text=departments of husbandry, they devote themselves to it with astonishing\r
#Text=constancy and alacrity, so as to form a complete contrast with their\r
#Text=characteristic indifference in most other respects.
#Text=This must, however,\r
#Text=be taken as a certain indication of the possibility of training them\r
#Text=up to useful labor; whenever they can be led on in a proper manner.
#Text=[Rice.]
#Text=Rice is, perhaps, of their agricultural products, the article\r
#Text=upon which the inhabitants of the Philippine Islands most depend for\r
#Text=food and profit; of this they have several different varieties; which\r
#Text=the natives distinguish by their size and the shape of the grain:\r
#Text=the birnambang, lamuyo, malagequit, bontot-cabayo, dumali, quinanda,\r
#Text=bolohan, and tangi.
#Text=The three first are aquatic; the five latter\r
#Text=upland varieties.
#Text=They each have their peculiar uses.
#Text=The dumali\r
#Text=is the early variety; it ripens in three months from planting, from\r
#Text=which circumstance it derives its name: it is raised exclusively on\r
#Text=the uplands.
#Text=Although much esteemed, it is not extensively cultivated,\r
#Text=as the birds and insects destroy a large part of the crop.
#Text=The malagequit is very much prized, and used for making sweet and\r
#Text=fancy dishes; it becomes exceedingly glutinous, for which reason it\r
#Text=is used in making whitewash, which it is said to cause to become of a\r
#Text=brilliant white, and to withstand the weather.
#Text=This variety is not,\r
#Text=however, believed to be wholesome.
#Text=There is also a variety of this\r
#Text=last species which is used as food for horses, and supposed to be a\r
#Text=remedy and preventive against worms.
#Text=The rice grounds or fields are laid out in squares, and surrounded by\r
#Text=embankments, to retain the water of the rains or streams.
#Text=After the\r
#Text=rains have fallen in sufficient quantities to saturate the ground, a\r
#Text=seed-bed is generally planted in one corner of the field, in which the\r
#Text=rice is sown broadcast, about the month of June.
#Text=The heavy rains take\r
#Text=place in August, when the fields are ploughed, and are soon filled with\r
#Text=water.
#Text=The young plants are about this time taken from the seed-bed,\r
#Text=their tops and roots trimmed, and then planted in the field by making\r
#Text=holes in the ground with the fingers and placing four or five sprouts\r
#Text=in each of them; in this tedious labor the poor women are employed,\r
#Text=whilst the males are lounging in their houses or in the shade of\r
#Text=the trees.
#Text=The harvest for the aquatic rice begins in December.
#Text=It is reaped\r
#Text=with small sickles, peculiar to the country, called yatap; to the\r
#Text=back of these a small stick is fastened, by which they are held,\r
#Text=and the stalk is forced upon it and cut.
#Text=The spikes of rice are\r
#Text=cut with this implement, one by one.
#Text=In this operation, men, women,\r
#Text=and children all take part.
#Text=The upland rice requires much more care and labor in its\r
#Text=cultivation.
#Text=The land must be ploughed three or four times, and all\r
#Text=the turf and lumps well broken up by the harrow.
#Text=During its growth it requires to be weeded two or three times, to\r
#Text=keep the weeds from choking the crop.
#Text=The seed is sown broadcast in\r
#Text=May.
#Text=This kind of rice is harvested in November, and to collect the\r
#Text=crop is still more tedious than in the other case, for it is always\r
#Text=gathered earlier, and never reaped, in consequence of the grain not\r
#Text=adhering to the ear.
#Text=If it were gathered in any other way, the loss\r
#Text=by transportation on the backs of buffaloes and horses, without any\r
#Text=covering to the sheaf, would be so great as to dissipate a great\r
#Text=portion of the crop.
#Text=It appears almost incredible that any people can remain in\r
#Text=ignorance of a way of preventing so extravagant and wasteful a mode\r
#Text=of harvesting.
#Text=The government has been requested to prohibit it on\r
#Text=account of the great expense it gives rise to; but whether any steps\r
#Text=have ever been taken in the matter, I did not learn.
#Text=It is said that\r
#Text=not unfrequently a third part of the crop is lost, in consequence of\r
#Text=the scarcity of laborers; while those who are disengaged will refuse\r
#Text=to work, unless they receive one-third, and even one-half of the crop,\r
#Text=to be delivered free of expense at their houses.
#Text=This the planters\r
#Text=are often obliged to give, or lose the whole crop.
#Text=Nay, unless the\r
#Text=harvest is a good one, reapers are very unwilling to engage to take it\r
#Text=even on these terms, and the entire crop is lost.
#Text=The laborers, during\r
#Text=the time of harvest, are supported by the planter, who is during that\r
#Text=time exposed to great vexation, if not losses.
#Text=The reapers are for the\r
#Text=most part composed of the idle and vicious part of the population, who\r
#Text=go abroad over the country to engage themselves in this employment,\r
#Text=which affords a livelihood to the poorer classes; for the different\r
#Text=periods at which the varieties of rice are planted and harvested,\r
#Text=gives them work during a large portion of the year.
#Text=After the rice is harvested, there are different modes of treating\r
#Text=it.
#Text=Some of the proprietors take it home, where it is thrown into\r
#Text=heaps, and left until it is desirable to separate it from the straw,\r
#Text=when it is trodden out by men and women with their bare feet.
#Text=For\r
#Text=this operation, they usually receive another fifth of the rice.
#Text=Others stack it in a wet and green state, which subjects it to heat,\r
#Text=from which cause the grain contracts a dark color, and an unpleasant\r
#Text=taste and smell.
#Text=The natives, however, impute these defects to the\r
#Text=wetness of the season.
#Text=The crop of both the low and upland rice, is usually from thirty\r
#Text=to fifty for one: this is on old land; but on that which is newly\r
#Text=cleared or which has never been cultivated, the yield is far beyond\r
#Text=this.
#Text=In some soils of the latter description, it is said that for a\r
#Text=chupa (seven cubic inches) planted, the yield has been a caban.
#Text=The\r
#Text=former is the two-hundred-and-eighth part of the latter.
#Text=This is not\r
#Text=the only advantage gained in planting rich lands, but the saving of\r
#Text=labor is equally great; for all that is required is to make a hole\r
#Text=with the fingers, and place three or four grains in it.
#Text=The upland\r
#Text=rice requires but little water, and is never irrigated.
#Text=The cultivator in the Philippine Islands is always enabled to secure\r
#Text=plenty of manure; for vegetation is so luxuriant that by pulling the\r
#Text=weeds and laying them with earth, a good stock is quickly obtained\r
#Text=with which to cover his fields.
#Text=Thus, although the growth is so rank\r
#Text=as to cause him labor, yet in this hot climate its decay is equally\r
#Text=rapid, which tends to make his labors more successful.
#Text=The rice-stacks form a picturesque object on the field; they are\r
#Text=generally placed around or near a growth of bamboo, whose tall,\r
#Text=graceful, and feathery outline is of itself a beautiful object,\r
#Text=but connected as it is often seen with the returns of the harvest,\r
#Text=it furnishes an additional source of gratification.
#Text=The different kinds of rice, and especially the upland, would no doubt\r
#Text=be an acquisition to our country.
#Text=At the time we were at Manila, it\r
#Text=was not thought feasible to pack it, for it had just been reaped,\r
#Text=and was so green that it would not have kept. [269] Although rice\r
#Text=is a very prolific crop, yet it is subject to many casualties, from\r
#Text=the locusts and other insects that devour it; the drought at other\r
#Text=times affects it, particularly the aquatic varieties.
#Text=There is a use\r
#Text=to which the rice is applied here, which was new to us, namely, as a\r
#Text=substitute for razors; by using two grains of it between the fingers,\r
#Text=they nip the beard, or extract it from the chin and face.
#Text=[Ducks.]
#Text=The market is a never failing place of amusement to a\r
#Text=foreigner, for there a crowd of the common people is always to be seen,\r
#Text=and their mode of conducting business may be observed.
#Text=The canals\r
#Text=here afford great facilities for bringing vegetables and produce to\r
#Text=market in a fresh state.
#Text=The vegetables are chiefly brought from the\r
#Text=shores of the Laguna de Bay, through the river Pasig.
#Text=The meat appeared\r
#Text=inferior, and as in all Spanish places the art of butchering is not\r
#Text=understood.
#Text=The poultry, however, surpasses that of any other place\r
#Text=I have seen, particularly in ducks, the breeding of which is pursued\r
#Text=to a great extent.
#Text=Establishments for breeding these birds are here\r
#Text=carried on in a systematic manner, and are a great curiosity.
#Text=They\r
#Text=consist of many small enclosures, each about twenty feet by forty or\r
#Text=fifty, made of bamboo, which are placed on the bank of the river,\r
#Text=and partly covered with water.
#Text=In one corner of the enclosure is a\r
#Text=small house, where the eggs are hatched by artificial heat, produced\r
#Text=by rice-chaff in a state of of fermentation.
#Text=It is not uncommon to see\r
#Text=six or eight hundred ducklings all of the same age.
#Text=There are several\r
#Text=hundreds of these enclosures, and the number of ducks of all ages\r
#Text=may be computed at millions.
#Text=The manner in which they are schooled\r
#Text=to take exercise, and to go in and out of the water, and to return\r
#Text=to their house, almost exceeds belief.
#Text=The keepers or tenders are of\r
#Text=the Tagalog tribe, who live near the enclosures, and have them at all\r
#Text=times under their eye.
#Text=The old birds are not suffered to approach\r
#Text=the young, and all of one age are kept together.
#Text=They are fed upon\r
#Text=rice and a small species of shell-fish that is found in the river\r
#Text=and is peculiar to it.
#Text=From the extent of these establishments we\r
#Text=inferred that ducks were the favorite article of food at Manila, and\r
#Text=the consumption of them must be immense.
#Text=The markets are well supplied\r
#Text=with chickens, pigeons, young partridges, which are brought in alive,\r
#Text=and turkeys.
#Text=Among strange articles that we saw for sale, were cakes\r
#Text=of coagulated blood.
#Text=The markets are well stocked with a variety of\r
#Text=fish, taken both in the Laguna and bay of Manila, affording a supply\r
#Text=of both the fresh and salt water species, and many smaller kinds that\r
#Text=are dried and smoked.
#Text=Vegetables are in great plenty, and consist\r
#Text=of pumpkins, lettuce, onions, radishes, very long squashes, etc.;\r
#Text=of fruits, they have melons, chicos, durians, marbolas, and oranges.
#Text=[Fish.]
#Text=Fish are caught in weirs, by the hook, or in seines.
#Text=The former\r
#Text=are constructed of bamboo stakes, in the shallow water of the lake,\r
#Text=at the point where it flows through the Pasig river.
#Text=In the bay,\r
#Text=and at the mouth of the river, the fish are taken in nets, suspended\r
#Text=by the four corners from hoops attached to a crane, by which they are\r
#Text=lowered into the water.
#Text=The fishing-boats are little better than rafts,\r
#Text=and are called sarabaos.
#Text=The usual passage-boat is termed banca, and is made of a single\r
#Text=trunk.
#Text=These are very much used by the inhabitants.
#Text=They have a\r
#Text=sort of awning to protect the passenger from the rays of the sun;\r
#Text=and being light are easily rowed about, although they are exceedingly\r
#Text=uncomfortable to sit in, from the lowness of the seats, and liable to\r
#Text=overset, if the weight is not placed near the bottom.
#Text=The outrigger\r
#Text=was very often dispensed with, owing to the impediment it offered to\r
#Text=the navigation of their canals; these canals offer great facilities\r
#Text=for the transportation of burdens; the banks of almost all of them\r
#Text=are faced with granite.
#Text=Where the streets cross them, there are\r
#Text=substantial stone bridges, which are generally of no more than one\r
#Text=arch, so as not to impede the navigation.
#Text=The barges used for the\r
#Text=transportation of produce resemble our canal-boats, and have sliding\r
#Text=roofs to protect them from the rain.
#Text=Water, for the supply of vessels, is brought off in large earthen\r
#Text=jars.
#Text=It is obtained from the river, and if care is not taken, the\r
#Text=water will be impure; it ought to be filled beyond the city.
#Text=Our\r
#Text=supply was obtained five or six miles up the river, by a lighter,\r
#Text=in which were placed a number of water-casks.
#Text=It proved excellent.
